items.correct: The external macro-environment: Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal and Environmental factors affecting a business.
items.misconception: PESTLE analyses the internal strengths and weaknesses of a business.
items.random_wrong: PESTLE stands for Profit, Earnings, Sales, Trade, Loans, Equity.
items.why: Students confuse PESTLE (external) with SWOT (internal + external). PESTLE is about the wider environment a business cannot control; SWOT mixes internal and external.

items.q: What is the purpose of a mission statement?
items.correct: To define the business's core purpose and values, guiding decisions and communicating identity to stakeholders — answering 'why we exist'.
items.misconception: A mission statement is a list of financial targets.
items.random_wrong: A mission statement is a description of what products the business sells.
items.why: Mission statements are qualitative and values-driven, not numerical or product-list. Students confuse mission (purpose) with objectives (specific targets) or product range.

items.q: Which sector does manufacturing belong to?
items.correct: The secondary sector — which transforms raw materials from the primary sector (farming, mining) into finished goods.
items.misconception: Manufacturing is in the primary sector because it makes things.
items.random_wrong: Manufacturing is in the tertiary sector along with services.
items.why: Primary = extracting raw materials; secondary = transforming them; tertiary = services. 'Making things' suggests primary to students, but only extraction is primary — building/processing is secondary.

items.q: Porter's three generic strategies are cost leadership, differentiation and:
items.why: Correct answer: Focus. “Diversification” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The 'Blue Ocean' concept was proposed by:
items.why: Correct answer: Kim and Mauborgne. “Porter” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many generic strategies did Porter identify?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 strategies. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
